title: Metrics & Monitoring — System Design
description: Designing a metrics and monitoring system: collect time-series measurements from thousands of services, store them efficiently, query them for dashboards, and alert when something breaks. The interesting problems are data-intensive: an enormous write volume of timestamped points, time-series storage with downsampling, controlling label cardinality (the silent killer), fast range queries, and reliable alerting. We build from a naive metrics table toward a sharded, downsampled, cardinality-guarded time-series platform with alerting.

nodes.label: Collector Agent
nodes.description: Collector Agent: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Alerting Engine
nodes.description: Alerting Engine: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, dashboard, or audit component.

nodes.label: Instrumented Service
nodes.description: Instrumented Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Query Cache
nodes.description: Query Cache: Fast derived state layer used to reduce latency or protect authoritative systems.

nodes.label: Cardinality Guard
nodes.description: Cardinality Guard: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Service Discovery / Target Registry
nodes.description: Service Discovery / Target Registry: control-plane component that discovers scrape targets, injects discovery labels, schedules scrapes, and tracks per-target scrape health.

nodes.label: Push Gateway
nodes.description: Push Gateway: accepts pushed samples from ephemeral/short-lived jobs that finish before a scrape, authenticated per tenant.

nodes.label: Control Plane
nodes.description: Control Plane: holds tenant/source auth, metric descriptors, cardinality budgets, retention policies, query limits, and alert routes.

nodes.label: Cold Store — rollups
nodes.description: Cold Store — rollups: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Dashboards
nodes.description: Dashboards: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Ingestion Service
nodes.description: Ingestion Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Notify — page/email
nodes.description: Notify — page/email: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Query Service
nodes.description: Query Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Ingestion Buffer
nodes.description: Ingestion Buffer: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Hot Store — recent
nodes.description: Hot Store — recent: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Rollup / Downsample Worker
nodes.description: Rollup / Downsample Worker: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Metric Router
nodes.description: Metric Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Alert Rules
nodes.description: Alert Rules: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, dashboard, or audit component.

nodes.label: TSDB Shard A
nodes.description: TSDB Shard A: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: TSDB Shard B
nodes.description: TSDB Shard B: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Time-Series DB
nodes.description: Time-Series DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

functional: Ingest time-series metrics (name + labels + value + timestamp) from many services, including histogram/distribution metrics.
Query metrics over time ranges with aggregation (rate, sum, percentiles via merged histograms/sketches, group-by label).
Render dashboards backed by those queries.
Evaluate alert rules and notify when thresholds/conditions are met, with silences and routing.
Retain recent data at full resolution and older data downsampled (preserving distributions for percentiles).
nonFunctional: Absorb a huge, continuous write volume (millions of points/sec) with a defined acceptance/partial-acceptance contract.
Fast range queries for interactive dashboards (sub-second for recent data) under admission control.
Bounded storage growth via downsampling and retention.
Control label cardinality so a bad label can't explode the index; per-tenant/per-metric budgets.
Reliable, highly-available alerting — don't miss a real alert; avoid storms of false ones; survive evaluator failover.
Graceful degradation: shed by priority under overload (keep alert-critical metrics) rather than fall over.
Multi-tenant isolation: per-tenant auth, cardinality budgets, and query limits.

capacity.label: Ingest rate
capacity.note: Thousands of hosts x many metrics x labels, every few seconds. Assume a 3x peak multiplier for deploys/incidents.

capacity.label: Sample-on-wire size
capacity.note: Delta-of-delta ts + XOR value compress a steady-interval sample to ~1.5 B in-chunk; ~12–16 B raw before compression.

capacity.label: Ingest bandwidth
capacity.note: 10M x ~1.5 B = ~15 MB/s steady, ~45 MB/s at peak (before replication). Pre-compression remote-write is ~10x that.

capacity.label: Raw write volume
capacity.note: 10M/s x 86,400 s = ~0.86 trillion points/day; at ~1.5 B/point that is ~1.3 TB/day before replication.

capacity.label: Hot store (raw, ~7 days)
capacity.note: ~1.3 TB/day x 7 days; multiply by replication factor (RF=3 -> ~27 TB) plus index/WAL overhead.

capacity.label: Rollup store (months)
capacity.note: 1m/1h rollups over months are orders of magnitude smaller than raw; retention cost is dominated by the raw hot tier.

capacity.label: Active series
capacity.note: A unique (metric, label-set) is one series; cardinality dominates cost.

capacity.label: Label-index memory
capacity.note: At ~1–3 KB of index/posting state per active series, 100M series is ~100–300 GB of in-memory index — the real cardinality ceiling.

capacity.label: Sharding
capacity.note: 30M peak points/s / ~1M points/s per node -> ~30 ingest shards; replicate each x3 for availability. Queue partitions ~= shard count.

capacity.label: Recent query latency
capacity.note: Dashboards need interactive reads over recent data.

capacity.label: Query envelope
capacity.note: Bound max returned series (e.g. 50k), max lookback at full res (e.g. 1h), and per-query timeout/bytes-scanned to protect shards.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: Old data is downsampled, not kept raw.

capacity.label: Write:read
capacity.note: Far more points written than queried, but queries scan wide ranges.

capacity.label: Alert evaluation
capacity.note: Rules run continuously over recent data; rule count x eval frequency sets evaluator fan-out and concurrency.

api.description: Push a batch of samples for a tenant. Acknowledged after admission to a bounded replicated buffer/WAL; the response reports partial acceptance so producers learn what was dropped. Histogram metrics carry explicit bucket bounds + counts so percentiles remain computable.

Range query with aggregation over a time window and step. Subject to admission control: max returned series, max lookback at full resolution, and a hard timeout; the response flags partial results when a shard times out and reports query cost.

Create an alert rule (expression + threshold + duration + routing + missing-data behavior). Owned by an evaluator shard; its evaluation state machine (inactive -> pending -> firing -> resolved) is persisted.
Suppress notifications matching a label selector for a window (planned maintenance, known noisy alerts) without disabling evaluation.

dataModel.name: series
dataModel.note: A unique (metric name + label set). The cardinality unit; indexed for lookup. Carries ownership and lifecycle so the system can enforce policy and detect stale series.

dataModel.name: samples
dataModel.note: Append-only (series_id, ts, value) points, stored in time-ordered, compressed chunks.

dataModel.name: histogram_bucket
dataModel.note: Per-bucket counters for a histogram metric so percentiles are computed by merging buckets across hosts (never by averaging per-host p95s).

dataModel.name: chunk
dataModel.note: A compressed time-ordered block of one series' samples; the unit of storage, compaction, and tiering. The label index points label matchers at series; series point at chunks.

dataModel.name: rollups
dataModel.note: Pre-aggregated lower-resolution samples (1m, 1h) for older data and fast wide queries. Histogram bucket counts are summed (not averaged) so percentiles survive downsampling; a version lets late/backfilled data recompute a bucket.

fields.name: version
fields.type: int (bumped on recompute for late data)

dataModel.name: metric_descriptor
dataModel.note: Control plane: per-(tenant,metric) policy — type, allowed labels, and the cardinality budget the guard enforces.

dataModel.name: scrape_target
dataModel.note: Control plane: a pull/scrape target discovered by service discovery, or a push-gateway registration. Failed scrapes are a liveness signal, distinct from 'value is zero'.

dataModel.name: alert_rules
dataModel.note: Continuously-evaluated rules, sharded across evaluators by owner so failover doesn't double-page.

dataModel.name: alert_instance
dataModel.note: The persisted state machine for one firing rule+labelset, so evaluator restarts/failover don't re-page or drop firing alerts.

dataModel.name: notification_attempt
dataModel.note: Evidence of delivery: each page/email send, its provider response, and retry/escalation bookkeeping so a failed provider doesn't silently drop a critical page.

dataModel.name: silence
dataModel.note: A label-selector suppression window; matched alert instances evaluate normally but don't notify.

patterns.name: Time-series storage (chunked, compressed)
patterns.what: Store samples in time-ordered, columnar, delta/double-delta compressed chunks per series.
patterns.whenToUse: Append-mostly timestamped data queried by range.

patterns.name: Downsampling / rollups
patterns.what: Pre-aggregate older data to coarser resolutions and drop raw points.
patterns.whenToUse: Bounding storage and speeding wide-range queries over long history.

patterns.name: Push vs pull collection
patterns.what: Agents push samples, or the system scrapes/pulls targets on a schedule.
patterns.whenToUse: Choose by service-discovery and network topology; pull eases liveness, push eases ephemeral jobs.

patterns.name: Cardinality control
patterns.what: Limit unique label combinations so the series index doesn't explode.
patterns.whenToUse: Always — high-cardinality labels (user id, request id) are the #1 way TSDBs fall over.

patterns.name: Pre-aggregation / read model
patterns.what: Materialize common aggregations so queries don't scan raw samples.
patterns.whenToUse: Dashboards repeatedly running the same heavy aggregations.

patterns.name: Rule-based alerting
patterns.what: Continuously evaluate expressions with a 'for' duration to debounce, then route notifications.
patterns.whenToUse: Turning metrics into actionable, non-flappy alerts.

patterns.name: Sharding by series
patterns.what: Partition series across nodes by a hash of the series id/labels.
patterns.whenToUse: When ingest/query volume outgrows one node.

patterns.name: Work queue / ingestion buffer
patterns.what: Buffer incoming samples so write spikes don't overwhelm storage.

steps.title: 1. Naive Metrics Table (the baseline)
steps.description: Start by writing every metric sample as a row in a relational table and querying with GROUP BY. It works for one service; at 10M points/sec a row-per-sample table takes ~0.86 trillion rows/day before indexes and retention — so it falls over, motivating a purpose-built time-series store.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why does a row-per-sample relational table collapse at metrics scale?

concepts.definition: A sequence of (timestamp, value) points for one metric+label set.
concepts.whyItMatters: Its append-mostly, range-queried shape is unlike OLTP rows.

concepts.definition: Each unique label combination is a distinct series.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cardinality, not raw volume, is what usually kills these systems.

talkingPoints: Name write volume, range queries, and cardinality as the three forces.
Reject a generic RDBMS for the sample store.

traps.trap: Storing each sample as a relational row with a btree index
traps.why: Index maintenance and storage explode; range scans are slow at billions of rows.
traps.instead: Use a time-series store: chunked, compressed, time-ordered per series.

bottlenecks.issue: Row-per-sample writes and indexes don't scale.

strong: Identifies cardinality and range-query patterns immediately.
weak: Treats metrics like ordinary OLTP rows.

whyNow: The baseline exposes why time-series storage and cardinality control are needed.

view.caption: The instrumented service sends each sample to the ingestion service, which INSERTs every point as a row into the time-series DB.

steps.title: 2. Ingestion Pipeline
steps.description: Accept samples fire-and-forget into a buffer, then write to storage asynchronously. Batching and a buffer absorb spikes and decouple producers from storage. Pin down the acceptance boundary (what `202` means), report partial acceptance, and define priority-based shedding so overload sacrifices debug/high-cardinality metrics before alert-critical ones.
steps.decisionPrompt: 10M points/sec arrive in bursts. How do you absorb them without blocking the services emitting metrics?

concepts.definition: Producers don't wait for durable storage; metrics are best-effort.
concepts.whyItMatters: Monitoring must never slow the systems it watches.

concepts.definition: Queue samples and write in batches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Amortizes write cost and smooths spikes.

concepts.definition: The point at which the system acknowledges a batch — e.g. after append to a bounded replicated buffer/WAL, vs after mere in-memory admission.
concepts.whyItMatters: It defines exactly what can be lost before `202` and what is durably retried.

concepts.definition: Under overload, drop low-value series (debug, high-cardinality) first and keep alert-critical metrics.
concepts.whyItMatters: Best-effort metrics still need a contract that protects the data alerts depend on.

deepDives.title: What does '202 Accepted' actually promise?
points: Acknowledge after appending to a bounded, replicated remote-write buffer/WAL so an in-flight batch survives a single ingestion-node crash; ephemeral agent-side buffers are explicitly best-effort and replay on reconnect.

Return partial acceptance: accepted/rejected counts, dropped series and reasons (cardinality_limit, out_of_order, bad_labels), and a retry-after when backpressured — so producers learn what was lost instead of assuming success.
Shed by priority under overload: keep alert-critical metrics flowing, drop debug/high-cardinality series first, and emit an owner-facing warning rather than OOMing the pipeline.
Make retried batches idempotent via an Idempotency-Key per batch so a producer retry after a slow `202` doesn't double-count counters; duplicates within a series+ts are de-duplicated.

options.name: Buffer + async batch writes (default)
options.description: Producers fire samples into an in-memory buffer/queue and ingestion batches them to the TSDB asynchronously, absorbing spikes without blocking writers.
pros: Absorbs bursts; batching maximizes write throughput.
Producers never block on storage.
cons: A replicated buffer/WAL adds cost; a purely in-memory buffer can still lose recent samples on node failure.
Partial-acceptance and idempotency handling add protocol surface vs a bare 202.

view.caption: The ingestion service drops samples into the buffer queue and batches them asynchronously to the TSDB, absorbing spikes without blocking.

options.name: Synchronous durable writes
options.description: Each sample is written straight to durable TSDB storage and acknowledged only once persisted, so the producer's write blocks until the store commits it.
pros: No sample loss.
cons: Slow path blocks producers; metrics shouldn't impose that.
Can't absorb spikes.

view.caption: The ingestion service writes each sample straight to the TSDB synchronously, acknowledging only after the store commits.

traps.trap: Making metric ingestion a blocking, durable write
traps.why: Monitoring overhead then degrades the very services it observes.
traps.instead: Fire-and-forget into a buffer; metrics are best-effort, not transactional.

traps.trap: No overload behavior
traps.why: A metrics flood can OOM ingestion and take monitoring down exactly when you need it.
traps.instead: Sample/shed under pressure and protect the buffer with limits.

failureDrills.scenario: Ingestion buffer saturates during an incident.
failureDrills.mitigation: Shed/sample low-priority series; keep alert-critical metrics flowing.

strong: Keeps ingest fire-and-forget and plans overload behavior.

recap.before: Samples hit storage synchronously per row.
recap.after: Buffered, batched ingestion that acks after replicated-buffer admission, reports partial acceptance, and sheds by priority under overload.
recap.newRisk: Best-effort agent buffers can still lose recent samples; the acceptance contract and shedding policy need tuning.

whyNow: A resilient intake path must exist before optimizing storage.

view.caption: The instrumented service reports via a collector agent to the ingestion service, which buffers samples in a queue before batched writes to the TSDB.

flows.name: Fire-and-forget ingest -> buffered batch write
flows.note: Producers don't block on storage: samples are accepted into a buffer and written in batches asynchronously; under overload the path sheds rather than stalls.

steps.title: 3. Time-Series Storage
steps.description: Store samples per series in time-ordered, compressed chunks (delta-of-delta timestamps, XOR-compressed values), with recent data in a hot store and an index from labels to series ids. This is what makes both writes and range reads cheap.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you store billions of points so both appends and range reads are cheap?

concepts.definition: Group a series' points into time chunks with specialized compression.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cuts storage ~10x and makes range scans sequential.

concepts.definition: labels -> series ids, so queries find matching series fast.
concepts.whyItMatters: Query selection happens on the index; sample scan happens on chunks.

concepts.definition: Latency distributions stored as bucket-count series (per `le` bound) or mergeable sketches (t-digest/HDR), not scalar values.
concepts.whyItMatters: Percentiles require mergeable distributions — you cannot average per-host p95s into a global p95.

deepDives.title: Why a TSDB beats a generic store
points: Timestamps compress with delta-of-delta; float values with XOR — both near-free for regular intervals.
Append-mostly per-series chunks turn writes and range reads into sequential I/O.
An inverted index maps label matchers to series ids without scanning samples.

deepDives.title: The storage mechanics you must name
points: A WAL/commit log makes the in-memory head durable: writes append to the WAL, fill an in-memory head chunk, and flush to immutable on-disk blocks; replay the WAL on restart.
Background compaction merges small blocks into larger ones, drops expired chunks, and builds block-level index/min-max-time metadata so queries skip irrelevant blocks.
Bound out-of-order samples to an acceptance window (e.g. last hour); older arrivals are rejected (counted in droppedReasons) or routed to a separate backfill path that recomputes affected rollups.
Index memory is the real ceiling: ~1–3 KB of postings per active series means 100M series ≈ 100–300 GB RAM — size the head/index before the disk.

Store histograms as bucket series (one series per `le` bound) so percentiles are computed by merging bucket counts across hosts; t-digest/HDR sketches are an alternative when bucket bounds aren't known ahead of time (mergeable, with bounded error).

traps.trap: Indexing every sample by timestamp in a btree
traps.why: Btree maintenance per point is the bottleneck; you want append-mostly chunks.
traps.instead: Chunk per series with columnar compression; index labels, not samples.

traps.trap: Keeping all data at full resolution forever
traps.why: Storage grows without bound and wide queries scan too much.
traps.instead: Downsample old data into rollups (next step).

traps.trap: Averaging per-host p95s to get a global p95
traps.why: Percentiles aren't additive — the mean of per-host p95s is not the fleet p95 and can be wildly wrong.
traps.instead: Store histograms (bucket-count series) or mergeable sketches and compute the percentile from the merged distribution.

failureDrills.scenario: A wide dashboard query spans 30 days at full resolution.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It stays fast and doesn't scan raw points across a month.
failureDrills.mitigation: Serve long ranges from rollups; raw only for recent windows.

strong: Chooses chunked/compressed per-series storage with a label index.

recap.before: Samples were rows with heavy indexes.
recap.after: Compressed time-ordered chunks + a label index make writes and reads cheap.
recap.newRisk: Full-resolution storage still grows; long ranges need rollups.

whyNow: Storage is the core of a TSDB; everything reads and writes through it.

view.caption: The buffer queue feeds the time-series DB, which keeps recent data in the hot store, and the query service does label lookups and scans chunks.

options.name: Purpose-built TSDB: compressed chunks + label index (default)
options.description: A custom engine stores each series as time-ordered chunks with delta-of-delta timestamps and XOR-compressed values, plus an inverted index from labels to series ids.
pros: Per-series time-ordered chunks with delta-of-delta timestamps and XOR-compressed values make appends and range scans both cheap.
A labels->series-id inverted index turns 'rate by service' into an index lookup plus a chunk scan.
Hot recent data stays in memory/SSD; older chunks flush to cheaper storage.
cons: Custom storage engine is complex to build and operate correctly.
The label index is the cardinality choke point — it must be guarded (next steps).
Schema is fixed to the time-series shape; ad-hoc relational joins aren't a thing.

view.caption: The buffer feeds a purpose-built TSDB of compressed chunks with a label index plus a hot store, which the query service scans for ranges.

options.name: LSM / wide-column store (Cassandra-style)
options.description: Samples live in a distributed LSM/wide-column store keyed by (series, time bucket), reusing its sharding, replication, and high append throughput for time series.
pros: Reuses a proven distributed engine: LSM writes absorb high append rates, and it scales horizontally out of the box.
Replication, sharding, and durability come for free instead of being hand-built.
Wide rows keyed by (series, time bucket) map reasonably onto the time-series access pattern.
cons: General-purpose storage doesn't compress time series as tightly as delta-of-delta + XOR.
Range scans read more bytes than a chunked TSDB, so wide queries are slower.
Still needs a separate label-to-series index layered on top.

view.caption: The buffer feeds a wide-column LSM store keyed by series and time bucket, fronted by a hot store, with the query service doing label lookups.

options.name: Relational table with a time-series extension
options.description: Samples sit in a relational table with a time-series extension that adds time partitioning and compression, keeping SQL, transactions, and joins available.
pros: Familiar SQL, transactions, and tooling; easy for teams that already run Postgres.
An extension (hypertables/partitioning) gives time-partitioned storage and decent compression.
Joins to dimension tables and arbitrary aggregations are straightforward.
cons: Row-per-sample overhead and index maintenance cap throughput well below a purpose-built TSDB.
Millions of active series strain the planner and bloat indexes.
Scaling writes past one node usually means manual sharding the extension doesn't fully hide.

view.caption: The buffer feeds a relational store with a time-series extension that the query service queries via SQL over time-partitioned, compressed data.

steps.title: 4. Downsampling & Retention
steps.description: Bound storage and speed wide queries by pre-aggregating older data into coarser resolutions (raw -> 1m -> 1h) and expiring raw points past a retention window. Queries pick the finest resolution that covers the requested range.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user asks for a 90-day CPU graph. What do you actually read?

concepts.definition: Aggregating raw points into lower-resolution buckets (min/max/sum/count).
concepts.whyItMatters: A 90-day graph needs hourly points, not millions of raw ones.

concepts.definition: Raw for days, 1m for weeks, 1h for months.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caps storage while preserving useful history.

traps.trap: Querying raw samples for long time ranges
traps.why: Scanning months of raw points is slow and expensive.
traps.instead: Pick the coarsest rollup that satisfies the step; raw only for recent, fine-grained views.

traps.trap: Downsampling by averaging only
traps.why: Averages hide spikes — a 1-second outage vanishes in an hourly mean — and discard the distribution percentiles need.
traps.instead: Keep min/max/sum/count per bucket, and for latency metrics roll up histogram bucket counts (summed, not averaged) so percentiles survive.

traps.trap: Treating rollups as immutable once written
traps.why: Late or corrected samples and backfills then never reach the coarse tiers, so long-range graphs disagree with raw.
traps.instead: Version rollup buckets and recompute affected buckets when late data lands or a retention policy changes.

deepDives.title: Late data, backfills, and percentile-safe rollups
points: A rollup bucket carries a version; when a late/backfilled sample lands in an already-rolled window, mark the bucket dirty and recompute it, bumping the version so caches invalidate.
Roll up histograms by summing bucket counts per bucket (mergeable) rather than averaging — that preserves the distribution so `histogram_quantile` stays correct at 1m/1h resolution.
min/max/sum/count survive spikes for scalar metrics; sketches (t-digest) are the rollup unit when you need approximate percentiles without pre-declared bucket bounds.
Retention-policy changes (extend/shorten a tier) trigger a recompute or re-tiering job rather than silently leaving inconsistent history.

failureDrills.scenario: An alert needs to catch a 5-second error spike but the query hits hourly rollups.
failureDrills.mitigation: Alerts evaluate against recent raw/fine data; rollups serve historical dashboards.

strong: Tiered retention and resolution-aware queries; preserves min/max.

recap.before: All data was raw and wide queries were slow.
recap.after: Tiered rollups bound storage and make long-range reads fast.
recap.newRisk: Downsampling loses detail — keep enough aggregates and alert on fine data.

whyNow: Without downsampling, storage and wide queries grow without bound.

view.caption: The rollup worker downsamples recent hot data into 1m/1h rollups in the cold store, and the query service reads raw for recent ranges and rollups for long ones.

options.name: Precomputed rollups + retention tiers (default)
options.description: A background worker pre-aggregates raw points into coarser tiers (raw -> 1m -> 1h) and expires raw past a retention window; queries read the finest tier covering the range.
pros: A rollup worker pre-aggregates raw -> 1m -> 1h, so a 90-day graph reads coarse points, not billions of raw samples.
Expiring raw points past a retention window bounds hot-store size.
Queries pick the finest resolution that covers the range — fast wide queries, accurate narrow ones.
cons: Rollups are extra storage and a background pipeline to run and monitor.
Aggregations are fixed at rollup time; an unanticipated function may need raw data you've expired.
Resolution boundaries can produce small visual artifacts when a query straddles tiers.

view.caption: The rollup worker pre-aggregates hot data into tiered rollups in the cold store, and the query service reads the finest tier covering each range.

options.name: Query-time downsampling (no precompute)
options.description: Only raw points are stored, and every wide query scans and aggregates the raw samples on the fly at read time with no precomputed tiers.
pros: No rollup pipeline or extra storage — only raw points are ever stored.
Any aggregation is possible at query time since raw data is always available.
Simplest pipeline: ingest, store, query.
cons: A 90-day graph must scan and aggregate billions of raw points on the fly — slow and CPU-heavy.
Keeping all raw data for long ranges blows up storage with no retention savings.
Repeated wide dashboard queries redo the same expensive aggregation every refresh.

view.caption: The query service reads raw points from the hot store and downsamples them on the fly at read time, with no precomputed tiers.

options.name: Tiered raw retention only (no aggregation)
options.description: Older raw chunks are tiered down to cheaper cold storage and the oldest are expired, but data is never coarsened — every read is at native resolution.
pros: Move older raw chunks to cheaper cold storage and just expire the oldest — simple lifecycle.
No aggregation logic; every point read is exact at native resolution.
Easy to reason about correctness and retention.
cons: Wide queries over cold raw data are still slow — you saved cost, not query time.
Storage shrinks only by deletion, not by coarsening, so long horizons stay expensive.
Cold-store scans for a 90-day graph read far more than necessary.

view.caption: Hot-store raw chunks age out into the cold store at native resolution, and the query service reads raw from whichever tier holds the range.

flows.name: Downsample raw -> rollups, expire raw
flows.note: A worker reads recent raw chunks, pre-aggregates min/max/sum/count into coarser buckets in the cold store, and raw points age out past their retention window.

steps.title: 5. Collection: Push vs Pull
steps.description: Decide how samples arrive: agents push to ingestion, or the system pulls/scrapes targets on a schedule via service discovery. This needs a control plane — a service-discovery/target registry that schedules scrapes, injects discovery labels, and tracks per-target health, plus a push gateway for ephemeral jobs and per-tenant auth so arbitrary clients can't mint unlimited labels. Many systems do both (pull for long-lived services, push for ephemeral jobs).
steps.decisionPrompt: Pull gives you free liveness checks but struggles with short-lived jobs. When do you push instead?

concepts.definition: The system periodically fetches metrics from discovered targets.
concepts.whyItMatters: A failed scrape is itself a liveness signal; central control of frequency.

concepts.definition: Targets send metrics to a gateway.
concepts.whyItMatters: Works for ephemeral jobs and networks where targets aren't reachable.

concepts.definition: The control-plane component that discovers targets, injects discovery labels, schedules scrapes, and records each target's last scrape status and owner.
concepts.whyItMatters: Collection identity and labels drive cardinality and tenant limits; a failed scrape is a liveness signal, not 'value is zero'.

deepDives.title: The collection control plane
points: Service discovery enumerates targets and injects labels (job, instance, team) the producer never sets — these become part of the series identity, so discovery directly shapes cardinality and per-tenant budgets.
Each scrape records up/down/timeout; treat a failed scrape as a distinct liveness signal (an `up == 0` series) rather than letting the metric silently go to zero or absent.
A push gateway covers ephemeral jobs that finish before a scrape and targets behind NAT/firewalls; it must expire stale pushed series so a finished job doesn't linger forever.
Authenticate and tenant-tag push ingestion so an arbitrary client can't mint unlimited labels or impersonate another team's metrics — push without auth is a cardinality and security hole.

options.name: Pull/scrape with a push gateway for ephemeral jobs (default)
options.description: The system scrapes targets on a schedule via service discovery, while a push gateway accepts samples from short-lived jobs that vanish before a scrape.
pros: Scrape failures = free liveness; central scrape control.
Push gateway covers short-lived jobs.
cons: Service discovery needed; firewalled targets are hard to scrape.

view.caption: The collector agent scrapes the instrumented service's /metrics endpoint on a schedule (with a push gateway for ephemeral jobs not shown).

options.name: Push-only
options.description: Every target, including long-lived services, pushes its own samples to the ingestion endpoint on its own schedule, with no central scraper.
pros: Simple for targets; works behind NAT/firewalls.
Natural for batch/ephemeral jobs.
cons: No free liveness signal; a dead target is silent.
Targets control rate -> harder to throttle centrally.

view.caption: The instrumented service pushes its own samples directly to the ingestion service on its own schedule, with no central scraper.

traps.trap: Pull-scraping short-lived batch jobs
traps.why: The job finishes before the next scrape, so its metrics are never collected.
traps.instead: Let ephemeral jobs push to a gateway; scrape long-lived services.

traps.trap: Ignoring missed scrapes
traps.why: A silent target may be down — the absence is signal.
traps.instead: Treat scrape failure as a liveness alert, not just missing data.

traps.trap: Unauthenticated push ingestion
traps.why: Any client can then mint arbitrary labels/series or impersonate another tenant, exploding cardinality and corrupting data.
traps.instead: Authenticate and tenant-tag producers; enforce per-tenant series budgets at the gateway.

strong: Picks pull for liveness, push for ephemeral, and uses both.

recap.after: Pull for long-lived services (free liveness) + push gateway for ephemeral jobs.
recap.newRisk: Service discovery and gateway add moving parts.

whyNow: How data arrives shapes liveness detection and the ingestion edge.

view.caption: Service discovery hands the collector agent its targets and labels; the agent scrapes long-lived services while ephemeral jobs push to the gateway, both feeding the ingestion service and buffer, and discovery reports per-target scrape up/down.

steps.title: 6. Cardinality Control
steps.description: Cardinality — the number of unique label combinations — is the silent killer of TSDBs. A single unbounded label (user id, request id, URL with ids) creates millions of series and blows up the index. Guard ingestion: drop, limit, or relabel high-cardinality labels.
steps.decisionPrompt: Someone adds a 'user_id' label to a high-traffic metric. What happens, and how do you prevent it?

concepts.definition: Count of distinct (metric, label-set) series.
concepts.whyItMatters: Memory and index size scale with active series, not sample volume.

concepts.definition: An unbounded label multiplying series.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the most common way these systems OOM and crash.

deepDives.title: Taming cardinality
points: Reject or strip labels with unbounded value spaces (ids, emails, raw URLs) at ingest.
Enforce a per-metric series limit; alert owners when a metric's cardinality spikes.
Offer aggregation at write time (drop the offending label) instead of storing every combination.

deepDives.title: Cardinality as an operator workflow, not just a drop
points: Enforce budgets at two levels: a per-tenant series budget and a per-metric budget from the metric descriptor, with allowlisted labels so only sanctioned dimensions create series.

When the guard acts, emit a structured report — offending metric, owner/team, tenant, the label name driving growth, estimated series added, and the policy action taken (dropped/relabeled/aggregated) — and notify the owner instead of silently dropping.
Support emergency overrides: a temporary budget bump for a legitimate new high-cardinality metric, and an allowlist entry so it isn't re-dropped on the next deploy.
Point owners at logs/traces/exemplars for the per-entity detail metrics shouldn't carry, so cardinality control redirects the need rather than just denying it.

traps.trap: Allowing arbitrary high-cardinality labels
traps.why: One bad label (request id) creates millions of series and OOMs the index.
traps.instead: Guard at ingest: cap per-metric cardinality, drop/relabel unbounded labels, alert on spikes.

traps.trap: Putting unbounded ids in label values to 'slice by anything'
traps.why: Metrics aren't a logging/tracing system; high-cardinality belongs in logs/traces.
traps.instead: Keep labels low-cardinality; use logs/traces for per-entity detail.

failureDrills.scenario: A deploy adds a label with per-request ids.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The index doesn't explode and the system stays up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cardinality guard drops/limits the new series and pages the metric owner.

strong: Treats cardinality as the primary scaling risk and guards ingest.

recap.after: Cardinality is bounded and monitored at the ingest edge.
recap.newRisk: Guard rules need tuning; legitimate new series must still be allowed.

whyNow: Cardinality is the failure mode interviewers most want addressed; it must be controlled before scaling out.

view.caption: Samples pass from the ingestion service through the cardinality guard, which forwards series within limits to the TSDB and drops or relabels high-cardinality ones.

options.name: Ingestion-side cardinality guard (default)
options.description: A guard at the ingestion endpoint drops, limits, or relabels high-cardinality labels before they reach the label index, enforcing per-metric series limits centrally.
pros: A guard at ingest drops/limits/relabels high-cardinality labels before they ever reach the index.
Protects the TSDB's label index — the actual thing that explodes — at the last choke point.
Centralized policy: one place enforces per-metric series limits across all producers.
cons: Dropping or relabeling at ingest silently loses detail the producer intended to send.
The guard itself must handle full ingest throughput without becoming a bottleneck.
Reactive: the offending label was already emitted and shipped over the wire.

view.caption: The ingestion service routes samples through the cardinality guard, which drops, limits, or relabels high-cardinality series before the TSDB.

options.name: Agent-side relabel / aggregate at the source
options.description: The collecting agent strips or buckets high-cardinality labels on the host before samples are sent, so explosive series never leave the producer.
pros: Strips or buckets high-cardinality labels (user_id, request_id) at the collector before they leave the host.
Saves network and ingest cost since the explosive series are never transmitted.
Producers keep ownership of what dimensions are meaningful to aggregate.
cons: Policy is distributed across thousands of agents — harder to enforce and update consistently.
A misconfigured or old agent can still flood ingest, so you usually still need a server-side backstop.
Local agents lack a global view of total series count per metric.

view.caption: The collector agent relabels or aggregates high-cardinality labels on the host, so only low-cardinality series reach the ingestion service.

steps.title: 7. Querying & Alerting
steps.description: A query service evaluates range expressions (rate, percentiles, group-by) over the index + chunks, with admission control (max series, max lookback, timeout) and caching for repeated dashboard queries. A separate alerting subsystem schedules and evaluates rules on fine data, runs each alert through an inactive -> pending -> firing -> resolved state machine persisted for failover, applies silences/inhibition/dedup, and routes notifications with retries and escalation.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep dashboards fast and stop a flapping metric from paging on-call every 10 seconds?

concepts.definition: Reuse results for recurring dashboard queries; materialize common aggregations.
concepts.whyItMatters: Dashboards re-run the same heavy queries on a refresh interval.

concepts.definition: An alert fires only if the condition holds for a sustained period.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents a momentary blip from paging on-call.

concepts.definition: Each rule+labelset moves inactive -> pending (condition true, within 'for') -> firing -> resolved, persisted so it survives evaluator restarts.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without persisted state, a restart/failover either re-pages firing alerts or forgets them.

concepts.definition: Caps on returned series, full-resolution lookback, timeout, and bytes scanned, enforced before fan-out.
concepts.whyItMatters: One expensive dashboard query can otherwise saturate every shard during an incident.

deepDives.title: Alerting that people trust
points: Evaluate rules on recent fine-resolution data on a fixed interval.
Require the condition to hold 'for' a duration to debounce transient spikes.
Deduplicate and group notifications; route by severity so on-call isn't drowned.

deepDives.title: Alerting as a subsystem (HA, state, delivery)
points: A scheduler runs each rule on its interval against recent raw/fine data; rules are sharded across evaluators by deterministic ownership (hash) or leader election so each rule has exactly one owner — and failover doesn't double-page because state is persisted, not in-memory.
Persist the inactive -> pending -> firing -> resolved state per rule+labelset so a restart resumes mid-'for' instead of resetting the debounce window or forgetting a firing alert.
Apply silences (planned suppression), inhibition (a cluster-down alert suppresses its dependent node alerts), and dedup/grouping before notifying, so one incident is one page, not a storm.
Notification delivery is its own queue with retries, escalation on no-ack, and a recorded delivery attempt per send — a failed provider must not silently swallow a critical page.
Define missing-data behavior per rule: alert ('monitoring data missing'), suppress, or treat as OK — so a vanished input series during an outage doesn't fail open.

traps.trap: Alerting on a single instantaneous breach
traps.why: Normal jitter pages on-call constantly; people start ignoring alerts.
traps.instead: Use a 'for' duration so only sustained conditions fire.

traps.trap: Re-scanning raw data for every dashboard refresh
traps.why: Identical heavy queries every 15s waste the cluster.
traps.instead: Cache query results and pre-aggregate common expressions.

traps.trap: Unbounded ad-hoc queries with no admission control
traps.why: A single 'group by everything over 90 days at full res' query can fan out to every shard and starve real dashboards mid-incident.
traps.instead: Enforce max series, max full-res lookback, timeout, and bytes-scanned; return partial results with a flag rather than running forever.

traps.trap: Keeping alert evaluation state only in evaluator memory
traps.why: An evaluator restart or failover then re-pages every firing alert or resets each 'for' window, hiding or duplicating incidents.
traps.instead: Persist the alert state machine and shard rules with deterministic ownership so exactly one evaluator owns each rule across failover.

failureDrills.scenario: A metric flaps above/below threshold every few seconds.
failureDrills.mitigation: 'for' duration + notification dedup/grouping suppress the flapping.

failureDrills.scenario: An evaluator node owning critical rules crashes mid-incident.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its rules keep evaluating and already-firing alerts aren't re-paged or dropped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Deterministic rule ownership reassigns the rules; persisted alert state resumes pending/firing windows on the new owner.

failureDrills.scenario: An input series disappears (target down) while a rule depends on it.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The system pages 'monitoring data missing' rather than silently going OK.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-rule on_missing_data policy decides alert/suppress/ok; default critical rules to alert.

strong: Debounces alerts with 'for' and caches/pre-aggregates queries.
Groups and routes notifications by severity.

recap.before: Data was stored but not usefully queried or alerted on.
recap.after: Fast cached queries and debounced, routed alerting.
recap.newRisk: Cache freshness and rule tuning become ongoing concerns.

whyNow: Storage is only valuable once it drives dashboards and trustworthy alerts.

view.caption: The query service answers range expressions over the TSDB with a query cache, while the alerting engine evaluates alert rules against the TSDB and fires notifications.

flows.name: Range query: recent raw vs rolled-up
flows.note: The query service picks the finest resolution that covers the range — raw from the hot store for recent windows, rollups from cold for long ranges — and caches repeated dashboard queries.

flows.name: Alert evaluation: schedule -> state machine -> notify
flows.note: The owning evaluator runs a rule on its interval against fine data, advances the persisted inactive->pending->firing state machine, applies silences/dedup, and hands a notification job to a delivery queue that retries and escalates.

steps.title: 8. Scaling by Sharding
steps.description: Shard series across nodes by a hash of series id/labels so ingest and queries scale horizontally. A metric router directs samples and queries to the owning shard; queries spanning shards fan out and merge. Replicate shards for availability.
steps.decisionPrompt: Ingest and active series outgrow one node. How do you split the load while keeping a series' data together?

concepts.definition: Hash the series id/labels to a shard so all of a series' samples co-locate.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps a series' chunks together for efficient append and range read.

concepts.definition: Fan a multi-series query to relevant shards and merge results.
concepts.whyItMatters: Queries spanning many series must aggregate across shards.

concepts.definition: A series fingerprint maps (via consistent hashing/virtual nodes) to a replica set of N nodes recorded in a membership registry.
concepts.whyItMatters: It defines ownership, how a node loss is survived, and how rebalancing moves only a fraction of series.

options.name: Hash-shard by series + replicas (default)
options.description: A router hash-shards series across nodes by series id/labels so each series lives on one shard; cross-shard queries fan out and merge, and shards are replicated.
pros: Linear ingest/query scaling; a series stays on one shard.
Replicas give availability and read scaling.
cons: Cross-shard queries fan out and merge; hot series/shards need rebalancing.

view.caption: The metric router hash-shards series across TSDB shards A and B so each series lives on one replicated shard.

options.name: Single big node + vertical scale
options.description: All series live on one vertically-scaled node, so ingest and queries hit a single machine with no routing or fan-out.
pros: Simplest; no routing or fan-out.
cons: Hard ceiling on ingest and active series; single point of failure.

view.caption: The metric router sends all series to a single vertically-scaled TSDB shard, with no fan-out across nodes.

deepDives.title: Hot shards and fan-out
points: A few popular metrics can make one shard hot — rebalance or split those series.
Multi-series queries scatter to shards and gather; push aggregation down to each shard first.
Replicate shards so a node loss doesn't lose recent data or block queries.

deepDives.title: Shard ring, replication, and partial failure
points: Ownership is a ring: the series label fingerprint maps to N consecutive nodes (the replica set). Membership lives in a shared registry so routers and queriers agree on who owns what.

Writes go to a primary plus replicas; for best-effort metrics, async replication or a low write quorum (e.g. ack on 1-of-3, replicate in background) keeps the ingest path fast while surviving a node loss.

Rebalancing on membership change moves only the affected fingerprint ranges (consistent hashing / virtual nodes) so adding a node shifts a fraction of series, not everything; dual-write or stream the moving ranges during handoff.
Hot-shard mitigation: split a hot metric across virtual shards, or adaptively route its sub-series, so one popular metric doesn't pin a node.

Partial query failure: when a shard times out, return partial results with a `partial: true` flag for dashboards, but fail closed for alerting (treat unknown as missing-data) so a flaky shard can't hide a real alert.

traps.trap: Sharding by time instead of series
traps.why: Every current write lands on one 'now' shard — a moving hotspot — and a series' data scatters across time shards.
traps.instead: Shard by series so writes spread and a series stays co-located.

traps.trap: Gathering raw points from all shards then aggregating centrally
traps.why: Ships huge volumes over the network.
traps.instead: Push partial aggregation to each shard; merge the small results.

traps.trap: Treating a shard timeout the same for dashboards and alerts
traps.why: Silently returning partial data to an alert evaluator can hide a real breach; failing a dashboard query outright is needlessly disruptive.
traps.instead: Return partial results with a flag for dashboards, but fail closed (missing-data) for alerting.

strong: Shards by series, pushes aggregation down, replicates shards.

recap.before: One node capped ingest and series count.
recap.after: Series-sharded, replicated cluster scaling ingest and queries horizontally.
recap.newRisk: Cross-shard queries and hot shards need fan-out and rebalancing.

whyNow: Sharding is the final lever once storage, cardinality, and queries are sound.

view.caption: The metric router hash-shards series across TSDB shards A and B, and the query service fans queries out to both shards and merges results.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: service discovery hands agents their targets (push gateway for ephemeral jobs); samples enter a buffered ingestion path that acks after replicated-buffer admission and is guarded for cardinality against per-tenant/per-metric budgets from a control plane; series are hash-sharded across a replicated time-series ring storing compressed chunks (with histogram buckets) and a label index; a rollup worker downsamples all shards into tiered, percentile-preserving retention; a cached query service answers dashboards (raw for recent, rollups for long ranges) under admission control; and an alerting subsystem evaluates debounced rules through a persisted state machine and routes notifications with retries. The diagram shows two illustrative shards; in reality the ring holds ~16–32 shards each replicated (RF=3), and both shards (all of them) feed the rollup worker.

view.caption: Service discovery + push gateway feed a buffered, cardinality-guarded ingestion path governed by a control plane (budgets, retention, query limits, routes); the metric router hash-shards series across a replicated TSDB ring; a rollup worker tiers all shards into cold storage; a cached query service serves dashboards under admission control and the alerting engine pages on rule breaches via a persisted state machine.

gcp.name: Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus

tradeoff: Self-hosted Prometheus/VictoriaMetrics/M3 give full control of compression, cardinality limits, and retention but you operate the TSDB, sharding, and replication yourself.
Managed Prometheus services remove the operational burden and autoscale storage, at higher per-sample cost and less control over internals.
TimescaleDB keeps SQL/joins but caps out far below a purpose-built TSDB at hundreds of millions of series.
makesIrrelevant: A managed, horizontally-scaling backend removes much of the hand-built shard ring and rebalancing logic from Step 8.

tradeoff: A durable, replicated log (Kafka/Pulsar/Kinesis) gives a replayable acceptance boundary and decouples spikes, but adds a stateful system to run.
An in-process WAL/in-memory buffer is simpler and lower-latency but only best-effort: recent samples can be lost on node failure.
Pull/scrape with service discovery gives free liveness and central control; push (Pushgateway/OTel) covers ephemeral jobs and NATed targets.
OpenTelemetry Collector standardizes pipelines across metrics/traces/logs but is another tier to size and operate.
Object storage (S3/GCS/Blob) is the standard cheap, durable tier for rollups and aged blocks; Thanos/Mimir/M3 query it directly.
Self-hosted object storage avoids egress/lock-in but you own durability and capacity planning.
A query frontend (Mimir/Cortex/Thanos) adds result caching, query splitting, and admission control in front of the shards.
Managed Grafana removes dashboard ops but still needs the query guardrails enforced server-side.
Alertmanager-style routing gives grouping, dedup, silences, inhibition, and provider integrations (PagerDuty/Slack/email) out of the box.
Cloud-native alerting is simplest if you already store metrics there, but offers less control over HA rule ownership and state.

functional.how: Query service over label index + chunks, cached.
Rule engine with 'for' debounce and routed notifications.

nonFunctional.how: Buffered async batch writes; series sharding.
Compressed chunks + label index + query cache.
Ingest-time guard: cap, drop, relabel high-cardinality labels.
Debounced rules on fine data with a persisted state machine, deterministic evaluator ownership across failover, silences/dedup, and notification retries.
Shed by priority under overload; keep alert-critical metrics flowing; partial query results for dashboards, fail-closed for alerts.
Per-tenant auth at collection, per-tenant/per-metric cardinality budgets, and per-tenant query limits from the control plane.

say: Restate it: collect, store, query, and alert on time-series at scale.
List functional (ingest, query, dashboards, alerts, retention) and non-functional (write volume, query speed, cardinality, alert reliability).
Name the three forces: write volume, range queries, and cardinality.
Size points/sec, active series, retention tiers, and alert cadence.
Sketch ingest, query_range, and rule creation.
Start naive to expose TSDB needs, then ingestion buffer, time-series storage, rollups.
Cover collection (push/pull), then cardinality control, then query + alerting.
Go deep where pushed: storage compression, cardinality, or alert debouncing.
Shard by series with replicas; push aggregation down on fan-out.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant risk (cardinality explosion).

expectations: Knows metrics are time-series needing a purpose-built store, not OLTP rows.
Understands dashboards query ranges and alerts watch thresholds.
Designs compressed chunked storage with a label index and tiered downsampling.
Treats ingestion as fire-and-forget with overload handling.
Makes cardinality control a first-class concern at the ingest edge.
Shards by series with fan-out aggregation and replication.
Quantifies storage/query/cardinality tradeoffs and degradation behavior.

followUps: How would you support high-cardinality exploration (exemplars/trace links) without exploding the TSDB?
How do you make alerting itself highly available so it never misses during an outage?
How would you implement long-term storage in object storage with a query layer on top?
How do you handle late-arriving or out-of-order samples?
How would you support multi-tenancy with per-tenant cardinality and query limits?
How do you backfill historical data and recompute rollups after a schema change?
